name: Ones and Twos
statement:
v>
You are given a $1$-indexed array $a$ of length $n$ where each element is $1$ or $2$.
Process $q$ queries of the following two types:
‚ÄĘ  "1 s": check if there exists a subarray$^{\dagger}$ of $a$ whose sum equals to $s$.
‚ÄĘ  "2 i v": change $a_i$ to $v$.
$^{\dagger}$ An array $b$ is a subarray of an array $a$ if $b$ can be obtained from $a$ by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the beginning and several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the end. In particular, an array is a subarray of itself.
input:
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains a single integer $t$ ($1 \le t \le 10^4$)¬†‚ÄĒ the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two integers $n$ and $q$ ($1\le n,q\le 10^5$)¬†‚ÄĒ the length of array $a$ and the number of queries.
The second line of each test case contains $n$ integers $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$ ($a_i$ is $1$ or $2$)¬†‚ÄĒ the elements of array $a$.
Each of the following $q$ lines of each test case contains some integers. The first integer $\mathrm{op}$ is either $1$ or $2$.
‚ÄĘ  If $\mathrm{op}$ is $1$, it is followed by one integer $s$ ($1 \leq s \leq 2n$).
‚ÄĘ  If $\mathrm{op}$ is $2$, it is followed by two integers $i$ and $v$ ($1 \leq i \leq n$, $v$ is $1$ or $2$).
It is guaranteed that the sum of $n$ and the sum of $q$ over all test cases both do not exceed $10^5$.
output:
For each query with $\mathrm{op}=1$, output "YES" in one line if there exists a subarray of $a$ whose sum is equals to $s$, otherwise output "NO".
You can output the answer in any case (upper or lower). For example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes", and "YES" will be recognized as positive responses.

samples.output:
YES
YES
NO
YES
YES
NO

note:
Consider the first example:
‚ÄĘ  The answer for the first query is "YES" because $a_1+a_2+a_3=2+1+2=5$.
‚ÄĘ  The answer for the second query is "YES" because $a_1+a_2+a_3+a_4=2+1+2+1=6$.
‚ÄĘ  The answer for the third query is "NO" because we cannot find any subarray of $a$ whose sum is $7$.
‚ÄĘ  After the fourth query, the array $a$ becomes $[2,1,2,2,2]$.
‚ÄĘ  The answer for the fifth query is "YES" because $a_2+a_3+a_4+a_5=1+2+2+2=7$.
